rulings.Case Title: LISA HABBESHAW VS. CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT /Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar on Thursday, March 13, 2014, Line 4, DEFENDANT HONORWAY INVESTMENT CORPORATION's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT/Adjudication. Granted. It is undisputed that Plaintiff tripped on a portion of the Market Street Bricks raised above the curb surface. (Undisputed Material Fact ("UMF") no. 11.) San Francisco imposes a duty on property owners to maintain the sidewalks in front of the property in good repair and condition. (Charter of the City and County of San Francisco, Section 7.601; San Francisco Public Works Code sec. 706(a).) However, the City and County of San Francisco ("CCSF") intentionally, knowingly, and repeatedly indicated that sidewalks comprised of the Market Street Bricks are an exception to this rule. Several Department of Public Work Orders provide that "[t]he property owner is responsible for the repair and condition of the sidewalk fronting his/her property except in the following instances: Special instances where City maintains sidewalks, e.g. Market Street Bricks¿" (Declaration of Matthew P. Guichard, Ex. E; Declaration of Scott H.Z. Sumner, Ex. C.) Additionally, Plaintiff previously argued in opposition to CCSF's motion for summary judgment that "[g]enerally a property owner is responsible for maintenance of their sidewalks; this is not the case with the [Market Street Bricks]." (Plaintiff's Opposition to CCSF's Motion for Summary Judgment, page 4.) Additionally, CCSF's conduct also indicated to Defendant that it was solely responsible for maintenance and repair of the bricks. CCSF has never charged or attempted to charge Defendant for repairs it performed on the bricks. (UMF no. 8.) Therefore, equitable estoppel bars application of a statutory duty here. To impose such a duty would be "intolerably unfair." (City of Hollister v. Monterey Ins. Co. (2008) 165 Cal.App.4th 455, 486.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure 437c(g) and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: DAVID LORENTZ et al VS. JPMORGAN CHASE BANK N.A., A NATIONAL BANK et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Omtion For Order Establishing Admissions And For Sanctions Against Rww Properties Llc;
rulings.Rulings: On calendar for Thursday, March 13, 2014, Line 1, DEFENDANT JPMORGAN CHASE BANK, N.A's motion For Order Establishing Admissions And For Sanctions Against Rww Properties Llc; Pro Tem Judge Katherine Huibonhoa, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Granted, for reasons set forth in defendant JPMorgan Chase Bank, N.A.¿s moving papers. There is no record that defendant RWW Properties, LLC opposes this motion. It is hereby ordered: (1) Chase¿s First Set of Requests for Admissions to RWW, Nos. 1 ¿ 11, are deemed admitted, with all objections, including privilege objections, waived. (2) Chase¿s request for sanctions of $2,064 is granted. Sanctions shall be paid within ten (10) calendar days of Notice of Entry of the signed Order on this motion. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to katherinehuibonhoa@paulhastings.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =302/jpt

rulings.Case Title: RADA KATZ et al VS. ALBERT W CHOW MD et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiffs Rada Katz & Oleg Gladshteyn'S Motion For Leave To File A Second Amended Complaint
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar on Thursday, March 13, 2014, Line 19, Plaintiffs Rada Katz & Oleg Gladshteyn's Motion For Leave To File A Second Amended Complaint. Denied. First, the Proposed Second Amended Complaint seeks to add causes of action for intentional and negligent emotional distress. On October 29, 2012, the Court sustained without leave to amend a demurrer on these same causes of action. Second, the motion does not conform to the Rules of Court. CRC 3.1324, titled "Amended pleadings and amendments to pleadings," applies to this motion. Plaintiffs have not stated what allegations are proposed to be added to the previous pleading, if any, and where by page, paragraph, and line number, the additional allegations are located. (CRC 2.1324(a)(3).) Plaintiffs also have not filed a supporting declaration specifying the effect of the amendment, why the amendment is necessary and proper, when the facts giving rise to the amended allegations were discovered, and the reasons why the request for amendment was not made earlier. (CRC 2.1324(b).) Third, Plaintiffs have not established they acted diligently in seeking amendment. Defendants have submitted evidence that billing information and informed consent agreements were produced pursuant to subpoena in 2011. (Declaration of John Ring, 3; Supplemental Declaration of John Ring, 8.) Plaintiffs' only explanation for not seeking to amend sooner is issues with the expert originally obtained in this action. Plaintiffs offer no explanation of why a different or additional expert was not obtained sooner. Further, Plaintiffs moved to augment their expert witness list in November 2013 to include their new expert witness and offer no explanation why they waited over three months to move to amend their complaint. Fourth, Defendants have demonstrated they will be unduly prejudiced by these amendments. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: ROBERT CABANISS VS. PACIFIC GAS AND ELECTRIC COMPANY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT /Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar on Thursday, March 13, 2014, Line 6, DEFENDANT PACIFIC GAS AND ELECTRIC COMPANY's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT/Adjudication. The motion for summary judgment is granted. The undisputed evidence demonstrates that plaintiff voluntarily resigned and the demotion did not cause plaintiff damage. Plaintiff cannot establish that he was constructively discharged. As a matter of law, the working conditions must be intolerable at the time of the employee's resignation to qualify as a constructive discharge. See Gibson v. Aro Corp., (1995) 32 Cal.App.4th 1628, 1637. In this case, the undisputed evidence demonstrates that plaintiff resigned before any new conditions were imposed upon him. rulings.Case Title: SAMUEL JAYME et al VS. SETON MEDICAL CENTER, A CALIFORNIA CORPORATION et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar on Thursday, March 13, 2014, Line 11, DEFENDANTs ATRIUM MEDICAL CORPORATION, MYCHALL MORRIS and JONATHAN LUNG's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Sustained. (CCP sec. 430.10(e).) As to the first cause of action, the demurrer is sustained with 20 days leave to amend. The First Amended Complaint ("FAC") does not plead facts establishing causation. As to the second cause of action, the demurrer is sustained with 20 days leave to amend. The FAC does not plead facts establishing that Defendants are physicians or surgeons, and therefore does not state a claim for medical battery. (BAJI 6.10.5.) The FAC does not plead facts establishing that Defendants intentionally did an act that resulted in a harmful or offensive contact with Patricia Jamye's person. (BAJI 7.50.) As to the third cause of action, the demurrer is sustained without leave to amend. The FAC alleges that Defendants are medical device representatives, not professional healthcare providers. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: SAMUEL RODRIGUEZ VS. LARRY FLYNT'S HUSTLER CLUB SAN FRANCISCO et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn To Compel Arbitration And Stay Action
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar on Thursday, March 13, 2014, Line 12, DEFENDANT SAW ENTERTAINMENT DBA LARRY FLYNT'S HUSTLER CLUB's Motion To Compel Arbitration And Stay Action. Motion to Compel Arbitration and Stay Action Granted. The arbitration agreement explicitly includes any and all claims arising from the employment relationship and arising under tort law. Plaintiff's claim for unlawful termination arises under the employment relationship and the claims for battery and the ratification thereof arise under tort law, and therefore both are within the scope of the arbitration agreement. Moreover, because the parties designated the FAA as the law governing the arbitration, CCP § 1281.2(c) is preempted by federal law. The selection of California substantive law as the law to be applied by the arbitrator does not affect the parties' agreement to fully incorporate the FAA, which lacks any provision permitting a trial court to refuse to enforce a valid arbitration agreement on the basis of pending litigation between the Plaintiff and a non-party to the arbitration agreement. Motion to take judicial notice of the complaint granted. rulings.Case Title: PATRICK KELLY VS. SHANE CLARIDGE KELLEY, AS EXECUTOR OF THE THOMAS F. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Mtn For Reconsideration Of Order On Demurrer
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar on Thursday, March 13, 2014, Line 14, PLAINTIFF PATRICK KELLY's Motion For Reconsideration Of Order On Demurrer. The motion for reconsideration or vacate is denied. The motion for reconsideration is untimely because the Court entered judgment. See Ramon v. Aerospace Corp. (1996) 50 Cal.App.4th 1233, 1236; APRI Ins. Co. v. Sup. Crt. (1999) 76 Cal.App.4th 176, 181-182. In addition, Plaintiff did not comply with the new law or declaration requirements of CCP 1008. Plaintiff fails to demonstrate a basis for relief under CCP 473. Plaintiff should have raised the reply issue at the hearing. rulings.Case Title: JENNY FARABEE VS. HEATHER MICHELLE GARRITY et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR CHANGE OF VENUE
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar on Thursday, March 13, 2014, Line 15, DEFENDANTs HEATHER GARRITY, ETHAN GARRITY and DOMINIC GARRITY's MOTION FOR CHANGE OF VENUE. Denied. The Notice of Motion only states that Defendants are seeking a change of venue based on forum non conveniens grounds. (CCP sec. 1010, "The notice of motion must state¿the grounds upon which it will be made.) "On a motion for forum non conveniens, defendant, as the moving party, bears the burden of proof." (Stangvik v. Shiley Inc. (1991) 54 Cal.3d 744, 751.) Defendants have offered no evidence establishing what witnesses will be testifying, explaining how a change of venue would be more convenient for them, or showing how the ends of justice would be promoted by a change of venue. (See Harden v. Skinner and Hammond (1955) 130 Cal.App.2d 750, 754.) rulings.Case Title: KEYENCE CORPORATION OF AMERICA, A CALIFORNIA VS. ANDREW NELSON et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion Of Subpoenaed Non-Party Witneses Cognex Corporation, Aaron Miner, Matt Remnek, Ivy Dewitt, Jeff Ball And Nick Vu To Quash Deposition Subpoenas, And For A Protective Order;
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar on Thursday, March 13, 2014, Line 17, Motion Of Subpoenaed Non-Party Witnesses Cognex Corporation, Aaron Miner, Matt Remnek, Ivy Dewitt, Jeff Ball And Nick Vu To Quash Deposition Subpoenas, And For A Protective Order. As to the motion to quash the subpoenas served by Keyence Corporation of America on Cognex Corporation and its employees, the motion is granted without prejudice to service of a subsequent subpoena tailored to discovery within the scope of a sufficiently described Section 2019.210 designation. C.C.P. Sec. 2019.210 applies to non-party discovery as well as party discovery in an action under the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (Cal. Civil Code Secs. 3426 et seq.). Opposing party did not meet its burden to demonstrate that the discovery was tailored to seek information specifically related to the designated trade secrets and finds that the PMK discovery and non-party depositions are overbroad and unduly burdensome as presented. See Perlan Therapeutics, Inc. v. Superior Court, 178 Cal.App.4th 1333, Advanced Modular Sputtering v. Superior Court, 132 Cal.App.4th 826, and Calcor Space Facility, Inc. v. Superior Court, 53 Cal.App.4th 216. As to the motion for protective order, the motion is denied. Plaintiff did make a designation under Section 2019.210 prior to initiating discovery. In view of the ruling on the motion to quash, the Court does not reach the question whether the designation designates the trade secrets with sufficient particularity. Plaintiff's request for sanctions is denied. The court will not provide a court reporter for this discovery hearing. If any party wishes a reporter, the parties must meet and confer and agree on a single reporter. rulings.Case Title: BELGRAVIA HOMEOWNERS ASSOCIATION VS. JOHN PILLETTE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Order To Show Cause Re: Preliminary Injunction
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar on Thursday, March 13, 2014, Line 20, Order To Show Cause Re: Preliminary Injunction. John Pillette is enjoined from: (1) performing any further construction work in Unit 203 without the approval of the Belgravia Homeowners Association ("BHA") pursuant to the Covenants, Conditions, and Restrictions (CC&Rs), Article VII, Paragraph 5; and (2) preventing BHA, accompanied by a San Francisco Sheriff, from entering Unit 203 on March 21, 2014 at 10:00 am in order to inspect any construction work pursuant to CC&Rs, Article VI, Paragraph 3. This preliminary injunction shall remain in effect until a final resolution on the merits of this matter is reached. BHA shall post a bond in the amount of $ 1,000 pursuant to CCP sec. 529 within five days of entry of this order. BHA's request for sanctions is denied.
